coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a new electrocatalytic sensor for determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid using synergic effect of NiO-SWCNT and 2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1- (2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline
coredata.dc:creator: Akbarian, Yousef
Shabani-Nooshabadi, Mehdi
Karimi-Maleh, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemical sensor developed for analysis of diclofenac by modification of carbon paste electrodes using NiO-SWCNTs as conductive mediator and 2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1-(2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline (DDPM) as electro-catalyst (NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE). We detected three completely separate signals at the potentials of ∼247 mV, ∼445 mV and 697 mV for oxidation of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid at the surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE which are sufficient for analysis of diclofenac in the existence of mefenamic acid and morphine. The electrocatalytic currents of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid are linearly with their concentrations in the ranges of 0.04–1200, 0.9–400, and 1.0–600 μM, with detection limits of 0.008, 0.4 and 0.5 μM at a surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE, respectively. The RSD% for seven replicate determinations of 15.0 μM of diclofenac, 45.0 μM of morphine and 80.0 μM mefenamic acid were 2.1, 2.5 and 3.1%, respectively. The NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE was used for the analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diclofenac
Mefenamic acid
Morphine
NiO-SWCNTs
Electrochemical sensor
2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1-(2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrocatalytic investigation of diclofenac at NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanism for electro-catalytic interaction of diclofenac at a surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE.
The structure of A) diclofenac; B) morphine and C) mefenamic acid.
A) XRD pattern of as-synthesized NiO-SWCNTs. B) SEM image of as-synthesized NiO-SWCNTs.

Cyclic voltammograms of (a) NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE in the buffer solution pH = 8.0; (b) 500 μM diclofenac at DDPM/CPE; (c) 500 μM diclofenac at NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE; (d) 500 μM diclofenac at NiO-SWCNTs/CPE; (e) 500 μM diclofenac at CPE. Scan rate 20 mV/s.

The Plot of electro-catalytic current vs. ν 1/2 in the presence of 200 μM diclofenac. Insert; cyclic voltammograms of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE containing 200 μM diclofenac at various scan rates; a–f correspond to 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 11.0, 17.0 and 25.0 mV/s, respectively.
A) SWVs of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE containing15.0 + 45.0 + 80.0 μM of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid at a surface of NiO-SWCNTs/CPE (A) and NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE (B).

A) SWV of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE containing different concentrations of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid (from inner to outer) mixed solutions of: (15.0 + 45.0; 80.0); (115.0 + 65.0 + 110); (230.0 + 90.0 + 140.0); (340.0 + 120.0 + 180.0); (480.0 + 150 + 220.0); and (570 + 170.0 + 245.0) μM diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid, respectively. B), C) and D) Plots of the oxidation current as a function of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid concentration, respectively.
Interference studies data for analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid.

row.entry: Alanine, valine, phenylalanine, Li+, Na+ F−, Br−

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of diclofenac, morphine, mefenamic acid in pharmaceutical samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a new electrocatalytic sensor for determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid using synergic effect of NiO-SWCNT and 2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1- (2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Nanotechnology, Quchan University of Technology, Quchan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study we reported first electrochemical sensor for simultaneous analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in drug and urine samples.

An electrochemical sensor developed for analysis of diclofenac by modification of carbon paste electrodes using NiO-SWCNTs as conductive mediator and 2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1-(2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline (DDPM) as electro-catalyst (NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE). We detected three completely separate signals at the potentials of ∼247 mV, ∼445 mV and 697 mV for oxidation of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid at the surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE which are sufficient for analysis of diclofenac in the existence of mefenamic acid and morphine. The electrocatalytic currents of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid are linearly with their concentrations in the ranges of 0.04–1200, 0.9–400, and 1.0–600 μM, with detection limits of 0.008, 0.4 and 0.5 μM at a surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE, respectively. The RSD% for seven replicate determinations of 15.0 μM of diclofenac, 45.0 μM of morphine and 80.0 μM mefenamic acid were 2.1, 2.5 and 3.1%, respectively. The NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE was used for the analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The diclofenac (Fig. 1 A) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) suggested for patients after surgery [1]. Due to high side effects of diclofenac such as drowsiness, nausea, dizziness, headache, abdominal pain, dyspepsia, rash and constipation, it is prescribed in the combination with other medicines especially morphine (Fig. 1B) and other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs such as mefenamic acid (Fig. 1C) and acetaminophen with low side effects after surgery [2]. The scientific investigation confirmed the consumption of combined NSAIDs such as diclofenac can be reduce the amount of morphine for patients after surgery [3]. On the other hand, the mefenamic acid and diclofenac overdose can be increased the risk of side effects and cause bleeding or bruising. Therefore, fast method for determination of above drugs in blood or urine samples are necessary for consumption control of drugs [4]. A few analytical methods were reported for determination of morphine in the presence of diclofenac [4–6] or diclofenac in the presence of mefenamic acid [7,8]. According to the best of our search, we cannot found any analytical procedure for simultaneous determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid. In between of analytical methods, electrochemical-based sensors can be useful for simultaneous analysis of drugs due to ability for modification with different mediators (for improving sensitivity and selectivity) and fast response [9–15]. The variety of conductive mediators such as DNA, nanomaterials, ionic liquids, inorganic mediators increased the range of compounds for electrochemical analysis and created a new ability for simultaneous determination of electro-active compounds in biological samples [16–25]. Therefore, in continuous of our group research [26–29], we tried for fabrication of a new electrochemical sensors amplified with NiO-SWCNTs as conductive mediator and 2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1-(2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline as electro-catalyst for simultaneous determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid. The NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE resolved the overlapping of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid signals and used for analysis of these drugs in pharmaceutical and serum samples.

Diclofenac, mefenamic acid and morphine were purchased from Merck. The graphite powder, nujol oil, diethyl ether were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used for fabrication of working electrode. The synthesis procedure and characterization of 2, 4-dimethyl-N/-[1-(2, 3-dihydroxy phenyl) methylidene] aniline was reported in our previous published paper [30].

Voltammetric measurements were performed on a μ-Autolab Type III connected to three-electrode cell (an Azar Electrode Company, Iran). Ag/AgCl/KClsat, NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE and Pt wire were used as reference, working and counter electrodes, respectively. KYKY-EM3200 digital scanning electron microscope (SEM) and PANalytical X’Pert Pro multi-purpose diffractometer (MPD) characterized the structure of synthesized NiO/SWCNTs.

NiO-SWCNTs was synthesized by chemical precipitation method according to reported procedure in our previous published paper [5]. NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE was prepared by disperse 0.9 g of graphite powder, 0.05 g NiO/SWCNTs and 0.05 g DDPM in 10 mL diethyl ether in mortar and pestle, and hand mixing by suitable amount of nujol oil after solvent evaporation.

Tablet of diclofenac (Darou Pakhsh Company, Iran), injection solution of morphine (APP Pharmaceuticals, LLC Schaumburg 0.5 mg mL−1), capsule of mefenamic acid and pharmaceutical serum were selected for investigation of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE ability for determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in real samples. The injection and pharmaceutical serum were used directly and without any pretreatment. The ten tablet of sodium diclofenac powdered and dissolved in water/ ethanol (1:1 v/v) mixture and filtered. The obtained solution diluted with PBS (pH = 8.0) and used for real samples analysis. The capsule was prepared in the same procedure with tablet sample.

The XRD and SEM methods were used for characterization of NiO-SWCNTs nanocomposite. The Fig. 2 A showed XRD patterns of NiO-SWCNTs nanocomposite which synthesized in the present work. The presence of miller indexes [1 1 1], [2 0 0], [2 2 0], [3 1 1] and [2 2 2] relative to NiO nanoparticle and miller index [0 0 2] relative to carbon nanotubes confirm the synthesized of NiO-SWCNTs nanocomposite (JCPDS cards No. 47-1049). Fig. 2B showed the presence of NiO nanoparticles decorated at single wall carbon nanotubes that confirmed the XRD pattern data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic investigation of diclofenac at NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface
ce:section.ce:para: The electrcocatalytic interaction for diclofenac at NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface was examined in the pH range 5.0–9.0 (0.1 M phosphate buffer solution). The results showed the maximum electro-catalytic reaction was occurred at a pH = 8.0 and it was used for the next experiments (Not shown).

In the similar conditions and at DDPM/CPE surface (curve b), we found out an electrocatalytic reaction between mediator and drug with lower oxidation signal, it is related to the absence of NiO-SWCNTs as a conductive material. The oxidation of diclofenac at a surface of unmodified carbon paste electrode showed a low oxidation signal (9.13 μA) with high over-potential (750 mV) (curve e). Although the oxidation signal of diclofenac increased to 21.4 μA after modification of CPE with NiO-SWCNTs (curve e), but it is not sufficient for determination of diclofenac in the low level. Therefore, simultaneous modification of carbon paste electrode with NiO-SWCNTs and DDPM is necessary for determination of diclofenac in the low level. The active surface area for CPE and NiO-SWCNTs/CPE were measured ∼0.284 and 0.312 cm2 that confirmed conductivity effect of NiO-SWCNTs in carbon paste matrix.

The dependence of electro-catalytic oxidation current response of diclofenac on scan rate was scrutinized at the NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface using various scan rates of 1–25 mV/s (Fig. 4 insert). The obtained oxidation currents gradually increased with increasing of scan rate and showed a dependency with ν 1/2 (Fig. 4) that indicates the diffusion manner for electro-catalytic oxidation of diclofenac at the NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface.

A Tafel plot was recorded using obtained data from cyclic voltammograms of 200 μM diclofenac at a surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE with scan rate 11.0 mV/s (figure S1; supplementary information data). Using slope of Fig. 5 and Tafel equation, we measured the value of α ∼0.54.

The chronoamperometric determination of 1.0 mM diclofenac at the surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE was done at the potentials of 400 and 0.0 mV as first and second applied potentials, respectively (Not shown). Using the Cottrell plots and slopes of them, the diffusion coefficient (D) value was measured ∼2.5 × 10−5 cm2 s−1. The rate constant of reaction between DDPM and diclofenac (kh) was determined ∼ 1.7 × 103 mol–1 L s–1 by the Galus plot and equation-using slope of current vs. t1/2 [31] (Not shown).

The stability of the NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE was tested for determination of 20.0 μM diclofenac by recording square wave oxidation signal of diclofenac for 10 cycles. The obtained results indicate that the anodic peak current of diclofenac are stable at the surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE that stability of proposed sensor. In addition, The RSD% for seven replicates determination of 15.0 μM of diclofenac, 45.0 μM of morphine and 80.0 μM mefenamic acid were 2.1, 2.5 and 3.1%, respectively that confirm good repeatability for suggested sensor.

In order to investigate the capability of the NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE for determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in the optimum condition pH = 8.0, the square wave voltammograms of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid were recorded in the different concentration ranges. The results show the anodic peak current of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid increases linearly by changing the concentrations in the range from 0.004 to 1200 μM (equation: Ip = 0.0427 Cdiclofenac + 11.1520) (figure S2; supplementary information data); 0.9 to 400.0 μM (equation: 0.0649 Cmorphine + 1.1816) (figure S3; supplementary information data) and 1.0 to 600 μM (equation: 0.0890 Cmefenamic acid + 0.5259) (figure S4; supplementary information data), respectively. The detection limits were calculated 0.008, 0.4 and 0.5 μM for diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid, respectively.

The square wave voltammogram of solution containing 15.0 + 45.0 + 80.0 μM of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid at the surfaces of NiO-SWCNTs/CPE and NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE are provided in Fig. 5. According to Fig. 6 insert voltammogram, the oxidation potential of above drugs cannot separate at NiO-SWCNTs/CPE and simultaneous determination of them is not possible in the absence of DDPM. Therefore, the square wave voltammograms of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid were recorded at a surface of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE. We observed three separated oxidation signals with potentials ∼247 mV, ∼445 mV and 697 mV at NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface that is sufficient for simultaneous analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid (Fig. 5) without any interference. The sensitivity for of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid at NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE surface and in the simultaneous analysis of them (Fig. 6 B, C&D) are similar to the obtained sensitivity for above drugs in dynamic range investigation. This point (did not changing in sensitivity for diclofenac in the absence and in the presence of other drugs) confirm the ability of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE for study of diclofenac in the existence of morphine and mefenamic acid.

The selectivity of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE for determination of 15.0 μM diclofenac in the presence of 45.0 μM morphine and 80.0 μM mefenamic acid was check in the existence of another biological and drugs materials with acceptable error 5% in current and potential. The results are presented in Table 1 and showed the NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE can be used for determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid without any important interference.

The tablet, injection and pharmaceutical samples were selected for study the capability of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE for analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in the real samples. The recovery data between 98.75–102.22 % for analysis of drugs obtained by standard addition method are presence in Table 2 and confirm good ability of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE for analysis of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid in the real samples.

The present study demonstrates the making of NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE by incorporating of NiO/SWCNTs and DDPM as modifying species in the carbon paste matrix. The NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE showed good capability for determination of diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid with oxidation potentials ∼247 mV, ∼445 mV and 697 mV for the first time. In addition, the presence sensor showed the detection limits 0.008, 0.4 and 0.5 μM for diclofenac, morphine and mefenamic acid, respectively. In the final step, the NiO-SWCNTs/DDPM/CPE was used for determination of drugs in the tablet, injection and pharmaceutical serum samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.06.049.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxic effects of the non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug diclofenac: part i: histopathological alterations and bioaccumulation in rainbow trout
Diclofenac sodium and mefenamic acid: potent inducers of the membrane permeability transition in renal cortex mitochondria
Morphine consumption in patients receiving rectal paracetamol and diclofenac alone and in combination
Application of modified multiwall carbon nanotubes paste electrode for simultaneous voltammetric determination of morphine and diclofenac in biological and pharmaceutical samples
A voltammetric sensor based on NiO/CNTs ionic liquid carbon paste electrode for determination of morphine in the presence of diclofenac
Application of spinel-structured MgFe 2 o 4 nanoparticles for simultaneous electrochemical determination diclofenac and morphine
Flow-injection spectrophotometric determination of diclofenac or mefenamic acid in pharmaceuticals
A molecularly imprinted polymer as the sorptive phase immobilized in a rotating disk extraction device for the determination of diclofenac and mefenamic acid in wastewater
Aptamer-functionalized nanoparticles for surface immobilization-free electrochemical detection of cortisol in a microfluidic device
A review: molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors for determination of Biomolecules/Drug
A highly efficient nanomaterial with molecular imprinting polymer: carbon nitride nanotubes decorated with graphene quantum dots for sensitive electrochemical determination of chlorpyrifos
Highly sensitive square wave voltammetric sensor employing CdO/SWCNTs and room temperature ionic liquid for analysis of vanillin and folic acid in food samples
Electrochemical determination of vitamin c in the presence of NADH using a CdO nanoparticle/ionic liquid modified carbon paste electrode as a sensor
Ultrafast immunoassays by coupling dielectrophoretic biomarker enrichment in nanoslit channel with electrochemical detection on graphene
Nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensing of neurological drugs and neurotransmitters
Liquid phase determination of adrenaline uses a voltammetric sensor employing CuFe 2 o 4 nanoparticles and room temperature ionic liquids
N‐(3, 4‐Dihydroxyphenethyl)‐3, 5‐dinitrobenzamide‐Modified multiwall carbon nanotubes paste electrode as a novel sensor for simultaneous determination of penicillamine, uric acid, and tryptophan
A novel DNA biosensor based on a pencil graphite electrode modified with polypyrrole/functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes for determination of 6-mercaptopurine anticancer drug
Palladium nanoclusters-coated polyfuran as a novel sensor for catecholamine neurotransmitters and paracetamol
Pencil-based renewable biosensor for label-free electrochemical detection of DNA hybridization
Electrochemical determination of carboplatin in serum using a dna‐modified glassy carbon electrode
Synergic effect of graphene quantum dots and room temperature ionic liquid for the fabrication of highly sensitive voltammetric sensor for levodopa determination in the presence of serotonin
Electrochemical determination of methyldopa by graphene quantum Dot/1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluoro phosphate nanocomposite electrode
Single-walled carbon nanotube-ionic liquid paste electrode for the sensitive voltammetric determination of folic acid
Functionalized graphene quantum dots with Bi-metallic nanoparticles composite: sensor application for simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid and tryptophan
Analysis of glutathione in the presence of acetaminophen and tyrosine via an amplified electrode with MgO/SWCNTs as a sensor in the hemolyzed erythrocyte
A nanostructure label-free DNA biosensor for ciprofloxacin analysis as a chemotherapeutic agent: an experimental and theoretical investigation

Simultaneous determination of 6-mercaptopruine, 6-thioguanine and dasatinib as three important anticancer drugs using nanostructure voltammetric sensor employing Pt/MWCNTs and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluoro phosphate
Voltammetric analysis of mycophenolate mofetil in pharmaceutical samples via electrochemical nanostructure based sensor modified with ionic liquid and MgO/SWCNTs
An electrochemical strategy to determine thiosulfate, 4-chlorophenol and nitrite as three important pollutants in water samples via a nanostructure modified sensor

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yousef Akbarian is a PhD student in Analytical Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran. His research interesting includes synthesis of metal based nanomaterials and application of them for development of chemically modified electrodes.

Mehdi Shabani-Nooshabadi is an associate professor on Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of Kashan, Iran. His research interesting includes electrochemical investigation of modified electrodes and corrosion filed.

Hassan Karimi-Maleh was born in Sari, Iran (1982) and received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from the Isfahan University of Technology (Isfahan, Iran) in 2011. He has published more than 180 research papers, many reviews and he is Top 1% Scientists in Chemistry and Agriculture simultaneously in ISI Essential Science Indicators. His research interest includes development of chemically modified electrodes and DNA sensors for biological, pharmaceutical and environmental compounds analysis and investigation of electrochemistry behavior of electroactive materials such as polymers, organic and inorganic compounds. Also, he focuses for synthesis of nanomaterials and application of them in drug delivery and sensor filed recently.
